jokes.text: Focus Group isn't the title of the movie. - We're in a focus group. - Huh?

jokes.text: We were walking out of the mall, and they offered us Red Lobster gift cards. Nevertheless, you know, they've chosen wisely.
jokes.setup: Dennis claims they were carefully selected for their expertise
jokes.punchline: Reveals they were just random mall-walkers bribed with chain restaurant cards
jokes.explanation: Classic Dennis delusion juxtaposed with the mundane reality. Good character work.

dialogue.line: We were walking out of the mall, and they offered us Red Lobster gift cards. Nevertheless, you know, they've chosen wisely.

jokes.text: See, I, myself, am a prolific filmmaker. In fact, while you don't thrill me, I might even consider you for one of my roles.
jokes.setup: Dennis establishing his supposed credentials
jokes.punchline: His condescending 'offer' to the moderator who just wants feedback
jokes.explanation: Pure Dennis narcissism - treating a focus group moderator like he's auditioning talent.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: See, I, myself, am a prolific filmmaker. In fact, while you don't thrill me, I might even consider you for one of my roles.

jokes.text: I'll get a performance out of you.

jokes.punchline: Dennis's creepy, vaguely threatening response
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical line that captures Dennis's manipulative nature. The implied threat makes it genuinely unsettling.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: I'll get a performance out of you.

jokes.text: Whatever piece of shit you're gonna show us, you know, so we can tear it apart and get out to Red Lobster?

jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures the gang's approach - dismissive arrogance combined with base motivations (free food).

dialogue.line: Whatever piece of shit you're gonna show us, you know, so we can tear it apart and get out to Red Lobster?

jokes.punchline: His casual racism followed by weak denial that it's racial
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy showing Mac's casual bigotry disguised as film analysis. The weak walkback makes it worse.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: In the old movies, the good guy wore white, the bad guy wore black, or was black. Well, depending on the hero. Right? It's-it's not a racial thing.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Mm. I hate people who are different than me.

jokes.text: Yeah. Look, I feel bad enough about myself as is. I don't need some super hot, funny, smart chick doing, like, roundhouse kicks and cracking computer codes to make me feel worse.

jokes.punchline: Mac's honest admission that competent women make him feel inadequate

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Yeah. Look, I feel bad enough about myself as is. I don't need some super hot, funny, smart chick doing, like, roundhouse kicks and cracking computer codes to make me feel worse.

jokes.setup: Mac explaining his preferred Thunder Gun formula
jokes.punchline: His clinical description of the disposable woman pattern
jokes.explanation: 'Bed 'em and dead 'em' is a memorable phrase. The casual 'she gets dead' is darkly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Bed 'em and dead 'em... first act, he meets a lady. She's super hot, but I'm smarter, right? And then he bangs her, and she gets dead.

jokes.text: Because all of the women are dead. You know? No competition.
jokes.setup: Mac's entire explanation about the Thunder Gun woman formula
jokes.punchline: His blunt admission that he wants all women dead to avoid competition
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy reveal. Mac's logic reaches its horrifying conclusion with perfect economy and shocking honesty.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Because all of the women are dead. You know? No competition.

jokes.text: Women hate women.
jokes.setup: Mac's misogynistic rant about Thunder Gun women

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Women hate women.

jokes.text: I mean, he's probably got a thousand kids, all the raw-dog loads he drops. I don't know what 'raw-dog loads' are.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie

jokes.text: Okay, so... where's the dong?

jokes.punchline: Mac cutting to what he sees as the real issue

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Okay, so... where's the dong?

jokes.text: John ThunderGun has hung dong in every chapter of this saga. And, well, I just watched 90 minutes of one, and yet not one hang.

jokes.punchline: His clinical analysis of the dong-per-movie ratio
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of the dong theme. 'Hung dong' and 'not one hang' become memorable phrases.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: John ThunderGun has hung dong in every chapter of this saga. And, well, I just watched 90 minutes of one, and yet not one hang.

jokes.text: Fuck you. - Bullshit! - This is exactly the type of Hollywood bullshit that America is sick of.

jokes.punchline: Gang treats this as outrageous censorship worthy of patriotic outrage
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation from mild suggestion to aggressive political stance. Shows how they turn everything into persecution.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Bullshit!

jokes.text: Years ago, before the Internet, we would all go out to the movies together... you give the creep-show at the window a couple of bucks, you could spend all day in there popping off.
jokes.setup: Frank nostalgic about community movie experiences
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's talking about masturbating in porn theaters
jokes.explanation: Frank's disgusting nostalgia presented as cultural criticism. The slow reveal makes the grotesque reality funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Years ago, before the Internet, we would all go out to the movies together... you give the creep-show at the window a couple of bucks, you could spend all day in there popping off.

jokes.text: And what am I supposed to do? Go home, turn the lights off and pop off in front of Charlie? No, thanks.

jokes.punchline: Reveals he's been talking about masturbating in public theaters
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection. Frank's seemingly philosophical speech about community is revealed to be about public masturbation.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: And what am I supposed to do? Go home, turn the lights off and pop off in front of Charlie? No, thanks.

dialogue.speaker: Frank

jokes.text: They did it! They did. They ruined everything with this PC bullshit. Straight white men can't have anything anymore.

jokes.punchline: Dennis frames penis removal as persecution of straight white men
jokes.explanation: Dennis turning movie rating into identity politics persecution complex. Perfect satire of manufactured outrage culture.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: They did it! They did. They ruined everything with this PC bullshit. Straight white men can't have anything anymore.

jokes.text: I'm mad, but I don't know at what anymore.

jokes.explanation: Charlie accidentally describes modern outrage culture perfectly. His confusion highlights how arbitrary their anger is.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I'm mad, but I don't know at what anymore.

jokes.text: I'm mad, but I don't know at what anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Did we leave a guy in there, though? Goddamn right we did, Charlie, and his name is John ThunderGun.

jokes.punchline: 'Thunder Son' as the new character name
